quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 8 Quiz 8
quiz.subtitle: Real-World Problems Using the Angle of Elevation
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 8.8 Applications of Trigonometry (Angle of Elevation)

questions.question: The angle of elevation is defined as:
options: The angle formed between the horizontal and the line of sight to an object above
The angle formed between the vertical and the line of sight to an object above
The angle formed between the horizontal and the line of sight to an object below
The angle at the top of an object looking down
questions.explanation: The angle of elevation is measured from the horizontal upward to the line of sight when looking at an object above the observer. It forms one acute angle in the right triangle created by the observer, the object, and the ground.

questions.question: A surveyor stands $100$ feet from the base of a building. The angle of elevation to the top of the building is $30^\circ$. What is the height of the building?
questions.explanation: Use tangent: $\tan(30^\circ) = \frac{h}{100}$. Since $\tan(30^\circ) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$, we have $h = 100 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} = \frac{100\sqrt{3}}{3} \approx 57.7$ feet.

questions.question: From a point $200$ meters from the base of a tower, the angle of elevation to the top is $42^\circ$. What is the height of the tower, rounded to the nearest tenth of a meter?
questions.explanation: Use tangent: $\tan(42^\circ) = \frac{h}{200}$. With a calculator, $\tan(42^\circ) \approx 0.9004$. So $h = 200 \cdot 0.9004 = 180.08 \approx 180.1$ meters.

questions.question: A lighthouse keeper spots a ship at sea. The lighthouse is $150$ feet tall, and the angle of elevation from the ship to the top of the lighthouse is $35^\circ$. How far is the ship from the base of the lighthouse, rounded to the nearest tenth of a foot?
questions.explanation: Use tangent: $\tan(35^\circ) = \frac{150}{d}$. With a calculator, $\tan(35^\circ) \approx 0.7002$. So $d = \frac{150}{0.7002} = 214.22 \approx 214.2$ feet.

questions.question: A person stands $200$ feet from a flagpole. The angle of elevation to the top of the pole is measured. The flagpole is $80$ feet tall. What is the angle of elevation, rounded to the nearest tenth of a degree?

From a point $300$ feet from the base of a cliff, the angle of elevation to the top is $25^\circ$. What is the length of the direct line of sight from the point to the top of the cliff, rounded to the nearest tenth of a foot?
questions.explanation: Use cosine: $\cos(25^\circ) = \frac{300}{L}$. With a calculator, $\cos(25^\circ) \approx 0.9063$. So $L = \frac{300}{0.9063} \approx 331.02 \approx 331.0$ feet. Alternatively, find height with $\tan(25^\circ)$ and then use the Pythagorean Theorem.

questions.question: Two observers are in line with a building. The first is at point $A$, and the angle of elevation to the top is $30^\circ$. The second is at point $B$, which is $50$ feet closer to the building, and the angle of elevation is $45^\circ$. What is the height of the building, rounded to the nearest tenth of a foot?
questions.explanation: Let $x$ = distance from point $B$ to the building. From $B$: $\tan(45^\circ) = \frac{h}{x}$, so $h = x$. From $A$: $\tan(30^\circ) = \frac{h}{x + 50}$, so $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{x}{x + 50}$. Cross-multiplying: $x + 50 = x\sqrt{3}$, so $50 = x(\sqrt{3} - 1)$. Then $x = \frac{50}{\sqrt{3} - 1} \approx 68.3$ feet, and $h = 68.3$ feet.

questions.question: When the angle of elevation of the sun is $40^\circ$, a tree casts a shadow $60$ feet long. How tall is the tree, rounded to the nearest tenth of a foot?
questions.explanation: Use tangent: $\tan(40^\circ) = \frac{h}{60}$. With a calculator, $\tan(40^\circ) \approx 0.8391$. So $h = 60 \cdot 0.8391 = 50.35 \approx 50.3$ feet.

questions.question: A plane is flying at an altitude of $10{,}000$ feet. A person on the ground spots the plane at an angle of elevation of $15^\circ$. What is the horizontal distance from the person to the plane, rounded to the nearest foot?
questions.explanation: Use tangent: $\tan(15^\circ) = \frac{10{,}000}{d}$. With a calculator, $\tan(15^\circ) \approx 0.2679$. So $d = \frac{10{,}000}{0.2679} \approx 37{,}320.6 \approx 37{,}321$ feet (about $7.1$ miles).

questions.question: A child flies a kite with $300$ feet of string let out in a straight line. The angle of elevation of the kite is $55^\circ$. How high is the kite above the ground, rounded to the nearest tenth of a foot? (Assume the string is tied at ground level.)
questions.explanation: Use sine: $\sin(55^\circ) = \frac{h}{300}$. With a calculator, $\sin(55^\circ) \approx 0.8192$. So $h = 300 \cdot 0.8192 = 245.76 \approx 245.7$ feet. The horizontal distance to the kite is $300 \cdot \cos(55^\circ) \approx 172.1$ feet.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered angle of elevation problems. You can set up the correct trigonometric relationship from real-world scenarios and solve for heights, distances, and angles with precision.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: the angle of elevation is always measured from the horizontal upward. Draw the right triangle, label the known and unknown sides relative to the angle, and choose SOH CAH TOA accordingly.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) identifying the opposite (height), adjacent (horizontal distance), and hypotenuse (line of sight), (2) using tangent when working with height and distance, sine or cosine when the line of sight is involved, (3) using inverse trig when finding the angle.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! In angle of elevation problems, the observer looks up from the horizontal. $\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{\text{height}}{\text{distance}}$. $\sin(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$. $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$. Draw the triangle and label carefully before choosing your formula.
